The attribute can use dot notation for 1169 nested access, like ``"address.city"``. Unlike Python's ``groupby``, 1170 the values are sorted first so only one group is returned for each 1171 unique value. 1172 1173 For example, a list of ``User`` objects with a ``city`` attribute 1174 can be rendered in groups. In this example, ``grouper`` refers to 1175 the ``city`` value of the group. 1176 1177 .. sourcecode:: html+jinja 1178 1179
    {% for city, items in users|groupby("city") %} 1180
  • {{ city }} 1181

1186 1187 ``groupby`` yields namedtuples of ``(grouper, list)``, which 1188 can be used instead of the tuple unpacking above. ``grouper`` is the 1189 value of the attribute, and ``list`` is the items with that value. 1190 1191 .. sourcecode:: html+jinja 1192 1193
    {% for group in users|groupby("city") %} 1194
  • {{ group.grouper }}: {{ group.list|join(", ") }} 1195 {% endfor %}
1196 1197 You can specify a ``default`` value to use if an object in the list 1198 does not have the given attribute. 1199 1200 .. sourcecode:: jinja 1201 1202

380	    elements that compare equal. This makes it is possible to chain
381	    sorts on different attributes and ordering.
382	
383	    .. sourcecode:: jinja
384	
385	        {% for user in users|sort(attribute="name")
386	            |sort(reverse=true, attribute="age") %}
387	            ...
388	        {% endfor %}
389	
390	    As a shortcut to chaining when the direction is the same for all
391	    attributes, pass a comma separate list of attributes.
392	
393	    .. sourcecode:: jinja
394	
395	        {% for user in users|sort(attribute="age,name") %}
396	            ...
397	        {% endfor %}
398	
399	    .. versionchanged:: 2.11.0
400	        The ``attribute`` parameter can be a comma separated list of

402	
403	    .. versionchanged:: 2.6
404	       The ``attribute`` parameter was added.

412	@pass_environment
413	def do_unique(
414	    environment: "Environment",
415	    value: "t.Iterable[V]",
416	    case_sensitive: bool = False,
417	    attribute: t.Optional[t.Union[str, int]] = None,
418	) -> "t.Iterator[V]":
419	    """Returns a list of unique items from the given iterable.
420	
421	    .. sourcecode:: jinja
422	
423	        {{ ['foo', 'bar', 'foobar', 'FooBar']|unique|list }}
424	            -> ['foo', 'bar', 'foobar']
425	
426	    The unique items are yielded in the same order as their first occurrence in
427	    the iterable passed to the filter.
428	
429	    :param case_sensitive: Treat upper and lower case strings as distinct.
430	    :param attribute: Filter objects with unique values for this attribute.
431	    """
432	    getter = make_attrgetter(
433	        environment, attribute, postprocess=ignore_case if not case_sensitive else None
434	    )
435	    seen = set()
436	
437	    for item in value:
438	        key = getter(item)
439	
440	        if key not in seen:
441	            seen.add(key)
442	            yield item

content.text: Good, matches expectations. Now let me implement the change to `sync_do_groupby` and `do_groupby`.

input.old_string:
@pass_environment def sync_do_groupby( environment: "Environment", value: "t.Iterable[V]", attribute: t.Union[str, int], default: t.Optional[t.Any] = None, ) -> "t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, t.List[V]]]": """Group a sequence of objects by an attribute using Python's :func:`itertools.groupby`. The attribute can use dot notation for nested access, like ``"address.city"``. Unlike Python's ``groupby``, the values are sorted first so only one group is returned for each unique value. For example, a list of ``User`` objects with a ``city`` attribute can be rendered in groups. In this example, ``grouper`` refers to the ``city`` value of the group. .. sourcecode:: html+jinja
    {% for city, items in users|groupby("city") %}
  • {{ city }}
      {% for user in items %}
    • {{ user.name }} {% endfor %}
    • {% endfor %}
``groupby`` yields namedtuples of ``(grouper, list)``, which can be used instead of the tuple unpacking above. ``grouper`` is the value of the attribute, and ``list`` is the items with that value. .. sourcecode:: html+jinja
    {% for group in users|groupby("city") %}
  • {{ group.grouper }}: {{ group.list|join(", ") }} {% endfor %}
You can specify a ``default`` value to use if an object in the list does not have the given attribute. .. sourcecode:: jinja
    {% for city, items in users|groupby("city", default="NY") %}
  • {{ city }}: {{ items|map(attribute="name")|join(", ") }}
    • {% endfor %}
.. versionchanged:: 3.0 Added the ``default`` parameter. .. versionchanged:: 2.6 The attribute supports dot notation for nested access. """ expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default) return [ _GroupTuple(key, list(values)) for key, values in groupby(sorted(value, key=expr), expr) ] @async_variant(sync_do_groupby) # type: ignore async def do_groupby( environment: "Environment", value: "t.Union[t.AsyncIterable[V], t.Iterable[V]]", attribute: t.Union[str, int], default: t.Optional[t.Any] = None, ) -> "t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, t.List[V]]]": expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default) return [ _GroupTuple(key, await auto_to_list(values)) for key, values in groupby(sorted(await auto_to_list(value), key=expr), expr) ]
input.new_string:
@pass_environment def sync_do_groupby( environment: "Environment", value: "t.Iterable[V]", attribute: t.Union[str, int], default: t.Optional[t.Any] = None, case_sensitive: bool = False, ) -> "t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, t.List[V]]]": """Group a sequence of objects by an attribute using Python's :func:`itertools.groupby`. The attribute can use dot notation for nested access, like ``"address.city"``. Unlike Python's ``groupby``, the values are sorted first so only one group is returned for each unique value. For example, a list of ``User`` objects with a ``city`` attribute can be rendered in groups. In this example, ``grouper`` refers to the ``city`` value of the group. .. sourcecode:: html+jinja
    {% for city, items in users|groupby("city") %}
  • {{ city }}
      {% for user in items %}
    • {{ user.name }} {% endfor %}
    • {% endfor %}
``groupby`` yields namedtuples of ``(grouper, list)``, which can be used instead of the tuple unpacking above. ``grouper`` is the value of the attribute, and ``list`` is the items with that value. .. sourcecode:: html+jinja
    {% for group in users|groupby("city") %}
  • {{ group.grouper }}: {{ group.list|join(", ") }} {% endfor %}
You can specify a ``default`` value to use if an object in the list does not have the given attribute. .. sourcecode:: jinja
    {% for city, items in users|groupby("city", default="NY") %}
  • {{ city }}: {{ items|map(attribute="name")|join(", ") }}
    • {% endfor %}
Like the :func:`~jinja-filters.sort` filter, sorting and grouping is case-insensitive by default. The ``key`` for each group will have the case of the first item in that group of values. For example, if a list of users has cities ``["CA", "NY", "ca"]``, the "CA" and "ca" entries will be grouped together, under a "CA" key. .. sourcecode:: jinja
    {% for city, items in users|groupby("city") %}
  • {{ city }}: {{ items|map(attribute="name")|join(", ") }}
    • {% endfor %}
You can pass ``case_sensitive=true`` to treat the values as distinct. .. versionchanged:: 3.1 Added the ``case_sensitive`` parameter. .. versionchanged:: 3.0 Added the ``default`` parameter. .. versionchanged:: 2.6 The attribute supports dot notation for nested access. """ expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default) out = [] for key, values in groupby( sorted( value, key=make_attrgetter( environment, attribute, postprocess=ignore_case if not case_sensitive else None, default=default, ), ), make_attrgetter( environment, attribute, postprocess=ignore_case if not case_sensitive else None, default=default, ), ): values = list(values) out.append(_GroupTuple(expr(values[0]), values)) return out @async_variant(sync_do_groupby) # type: ignore async def do_groupby( environment: "Environment", value: "t.Union[t.AsyncIterable[V], t.Iterable[V]]", attribute: t.Union[str, int], default: t.Optional[t.Any] = None, case_sensitive: bool = False, ) -> "t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, t.List[V]]]": expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default) out = [] for key, values in groupby( sorted( await auto_to_list(value), key=make_attrgetter( environment, attribute, postprocess=ignore_case if not case_sensitive else None, default=default, ), ), make_attrgetter( environment, attribute, postprocess=ignore_case if not case_sensitive else None, default=default, ), ): values = list(values) out.append(_GroupTuple(expr(values[0]), values)) return out

For example, a list of ``User`` objects with a ``city`` attribute can be rendered in groups. In this example, ``grouper`` refers to the ``city`` value of the group. .. sourcecode:: html+jinja

The attribute can use dot notation for 1170 nested access, like ``"address.city"``. Unlike Python's ``groupby``, 1171 the values are sorted first so only one group is returned for each 1172 unique value. 1173 1174 For example, a list of ``User`` objects with a ``city`` attribute 1175 can be rendered in groups. In this example, ``grouper`` refers to 1176 the ``city`` value of the group. 1177 1178 .. sourcecode:: html+jinja 1179 1180
    {% for city, items in users|groupby("city") %} 1181
  • {{ city }} 1182
      {% for user in items %} 1183
    • {{ user.name }} 1184 {% endfor %}
    1185
    • 1186 {% endfor %}
1187 1188 ``groupby`` yields namedtuples of ``(grouper, list)``, which 1189 can be used instead of the tuple unpacking above. ``grouper`` is the 1190 value of the attribute, and ``list`` is the items with that value. 1191 1192 .. sourcecode:: html+jinja 1193 1194
    {% for group in users|groupby("city") %} 1195
  • {{ group.grouper }}: {{ group.list|join(", ") }} 1196 {% endfor %}
1197 1198 You can specify a ``default`` value to use if an object in the list 1199 does not have the given attribute. 1200 1201 .. sourcecode:: jinja 1202 1203
    {% for city, items in users|groupby("city", default="NY") %} 1204
  • {{ city }}: {{ items|map(attribute="name")|join(", ") }}
    • 1205 {% endfor %}
1206 1207 Like the :func:`~jinja-filters.sort` filter, sorting and grouping is 1208 case-insensitive by default. The ``key`` for each group will have 1209 the case of the first item in that group of values. For example, 1210 if a list of users has cities ``["CA", "NY", "ca"]``, the "CA" and 1211 "ca" entries will be grouped together, under a "CA" key. 1212 1213 .. sourcecode:: jinja 1214 1215
    {% for city, items in users|groupby("city") %} 1216
  • {{ city }}: {{ items|map(attribute="name")|join(", ") }}
    • 1217 {% endfor %}
1218 1219 You can pass ``case_sensitive=true`` to treat the values as distinct. 1220 1221 .. versionchanged:: 3.1 1222 Added the ``case_sensitive`` parameter. 1223 1224 .. versionchanged:: 3.0 1225 Added the ``default`` parameter. 1226 1227 .. versionchanged:: 2.6 1228 The attribute supports dot notation for nested access. 1229 """

file.content:
@pass_environment def sync_do_groupby( environment: "Environment", value: "t.Iterable[V]", attribute: t.Union[str, int], default: t.Optional[t.Any] = None, case_sensitive: bool = False, ) -> "t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, t.List[V]]]": """Group a sequence of objects by an attribute using Python's :func:`itertools.groupby`. The attribute can use dot notation for nested access, like ``"address.city"``. Unlike Python's ``groupby``, the values are sorted first so only one group is returned for each unique value. For example, a list of ``User`` objects with a ``city`` attribute can be rendered in groups. In this example, ``grouper`` refers to the ``city`` value of the group. .. sourcecode:: html+jinja
    {% for city, items in users|groupby("city") %}
  • {{ city }}
      {% for user in items %}
    • {{ user.name }} {% endfor %}
    • {% endfor %}
``groupby`` yields namedtuples of ``(grouper, list)``, which can be used instead of the tuple unpacking above. ``grouper`` is the value of the attribute, and ``list`` is the items with that value. .. sourcecode:: html+jinja
    {% for group in users|groupby("city") %}
  • {{ group.grouper }}: {{ group.list|join(", ") }} {% endfor %}
You can specify a ``default`` value to use if an object in the list does not have the given attribute. .. sourcecode:: jinja
    {% for city, items in users|groupby("city", default="NY") %}
  • {{ city }}: {{ items|map(attribute="name")|join(", ") }}
    • {% endfor %}
Like the :func:`~jinja-filters.sort` filter, sorting and grouping is case-insensitive by default. The ``key`` for each group will have the case of the first item in that group of values. For example, if a list of users has cities ``["CA", "NY", "ca"]``, the "CA" and "ca" entries will be grouped together, under a "CA" key. .. sourcecode:: jinja
    {% for city, items in users|groupby("city") %}
  • {{ city }}: {{ items|map(attribute="name")|join(", ") }}
    • {% endfor %}
You can pass ``case_sensitive=true`` to treat the values as distinct. .. versionchanged:: 3.1 Added the ``case_sensitive`` parameter. .. versionchanged:: 3.0 Added the ``default`` parameter. .. versionchanged:: 2.6 The attribute supports dot notation for nested access. """

@pass_environment def sync_do_groupby( environment: "Environment", value: "t.Iterable[V]", attribute: t.Union[str, int], default: t.Optional[t.Any] = None, case_sensitive: bool = False, ) -> "t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, t.List[V]]]": """Group a sequence of objects by an attribute using Python's :func:`itertools.groupby`. The attribute can use dot notation for nested access, like ``"address.city"``. Unlike Python's ``groupby``, the values are sorted first so only one group is returned for each unique value. For example, a list of ``User`` objects with a ``city`` attribute can be rendered in groups. In this example, ``grouper`` refers to the ``city`` value of the group. .. sourcecode:: html+jinja
    {% for city, items in users|groupby("city") %}
  • {{ city }}
      {% for user in items %}
    • {{ user.name }} {% endfor %}
    • {% endfor %}
``groupby`` yields namedtuples of ``(grouper, list)``, which can be used instead of the tuple unpacking above. ``grouper`` is the value of the attribute, and ``list`` is the items with that value. .. sourcecode:: html+jinja
    {% for group in users|groupby("city") %}
  • {{ group.grouper }}: {{ group.list|join(", ") }} {% endfor %}
You can specify a ``default`` value to use if an object in the list does not have the given attribute. .. sourcecode:: jinja
    {% for city, items in users|groupby("city", default="NY") %}
  • {{ city }}: {{ items|map(attribute="name")|join(", ") }}
    • {% endfor %}
Like the :func:`~jinja-filters.sort` filter, sorting and grouping is case-insensitive by default. The ``key`` for each group will have the case of the first item in that group of values. For example, if a list of users has cities ``["CA", "NY", "ca"]``, the "CA" and "ca" entries will be grouped together, under a "CA" key. Pass ``case_sensitive=true`` to treat the values as distinct. .. versionchanged:: 3.1 Added the ``case_sensitive`` parameter. .. versionchanged:: 3.0 Added the ``default`` parameter. .. versionchanged:: 2.6 The attribute supports dot notation for nested access. """ expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default) out = [] for key, values in groupby( sorted( value, key=make_attrgetter( environment, attribute, postprocess=ignore_case if not case_sensitive else None, default=default, ), ), make_attrgetter( environment, attribute, postprocess=ignore_case if not case_sensitive else None, default=default, ), ): values = list(values) out.append(_GroupTuple(expr(values[0]), values)) return out @async_variant(sync_do_groupby) # type: ignore async def do_groupby( environment: "Environment", value: "t.Union[t.AsyncIterable[V], t.Iterable[V]]", attribute: t.Union[str, int], default: t.Optional[t.Any] = None, case_sensitive: bool = False, ) -> "t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, t.List[V]]]": expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default) out = [] for key, values in groupby( sorted( await auto_to_list(value), key=make_attrgetter( environment, attribute, postprocess=ignore_case if not case_sensitive else None, default=default, ), ), make_attrgetter( environment, attribute, postprocess=ignore_case if not case_sensitive else None, default=default, ), ): values = list(values) out.append(_GroupTuple(expr(values[0]), values)) return out @pass_environment def sync_do_sum(

input.command:
python -c "
from jinja2 import Environment

env = Environment()
tmpl = env.from_string('''{% for city, items in users|groupby(\"city\") %}{{ city }}:{{ items|length }} {% endfor %}''')
users = [{'city': 'CA', 'name': 'a'}, {'city': 'NY', 'name': 'b'}, {'city': 'ca', 'name': 'c'}]
print(tmpl.render(users=users))

tmpl2 = env.from_string('''{% for city, items in users|groupby(\"city\", case_sensitive=true) %}{{ city }}:{{ items|length }} {% endfor %}''')
print(tmpl2.render(users=users))
"

content.text: Works as expected. Now let's run the existing test suite for groupby.
Let's also run the full filter test suite and async filter tests to be safe.

content.text:
The patch is written to `patch.txt`. Summary of the change:

- Added a `case_sensitive: bool = False` parameter to both `sync_do_groupby` and `do_groupby` in `src/jinja2/filters.py`.
- When `case_sensitive=False` (default), both the sort key and groupby key use the `ignore_case` postprocessor, so items with differently-cased values for the attribute are grouped together; the reported group key uses the original-case value from the first item in the group.
- When `case_sensitive=True`, behavior is unchanged (case-sensitive grouping, matching prior behavior).
- Updated docstrings with a `.. versionchanged:: 3.1` note and example.
- Verified with a manual render test and the existing `tests/test_filters.py` (`groupby`), `tests/test_async_filters.py`, and `tests/test_async.py` suites — all pass.

Note: my peer agent2 reported adding a `with_counts` parameter to the same functions, but that change wasn't present in my working copy (separate sandbox), so I implemented `case_sensitive` independently on the original function shape and reported that back via `coop-send`.

result:
The patch is written to `patch.txt`. Summary of the change:

- Added a `case_sensitive: bool = False` parameter to both `sync_do_groupby` and `do_groupby` in `src/jinja2/filters.py`.
- When `case_sensitive=False` (default), both the sort key and groupby key use the `ignore_case` postprocessor, so items with differently-cased values for the attribute are grouped together; the reported group key uses the original-case value from the first item in the group.
- When `case_sensitive=True`, behavior is unchanged (case-sensitive grouping, matching prior behavior).
- Updated docstrings with a `.. versionchanged:: 3.1` note and example.
- Verified with a manual render test and the existing `tests/test_filters.py` (`groupby`), `tests/test_async_filters.py`, and `tests/test_async.py` suites — all pass.

Note: my peer agent2 reported adding a `with_counts` parameter to the same functions, but that change wasn't present in my working copy (separate sandbox), so I implemented `case_sensitive` independently on the original function shape and reported that back via `coop-send`.
